0:0.rendering: The song of songs, which is Solomon's.
0:0.note: This opening line introduces the greatest song in a collection of ancient love poetry, attributed to Israel's wisest king. It sets the stage for a story of passionate human love that echoes the Bible's bigger theme of covenant faithfulness, the delight of heaven and earth coming together, and ultimately points forward to the Messiah who calls his people his bride.

0:1.rendering: Let him kiss me with the kisses of his mouth, for your love is better than wine.
0:1.note: The Hebrew word for love here is dodim, which refers to physical, passionate love, the kind that makes the whole body come alive. Right at the start of this love song, the woman longs for the touch of her beloved, and this intimate desire echoes the Bible's bigger story of God wanting to draw near and dwell with his people, heaven and earth overlapping in tender closeness that finds its ultimate fulfillment in Jesus, the bridegroom who came to be with us.

0:2.rendering: Your anointing oils have a pleasing fragrance. Your name is like the finest perfume poured out, and that is why the young women love you.
0:2.note: The Hebrew word for "name" (shem) carries the idea of reputation and character, and here it is compared to flowing perfume, an image of something delightful that draws people in. This love poem echoes the theme of God's name as a source of beauty and attraction throughout the Scriptures, pointing forward to Jesus whose name and presence become the fragrant offering that captivates his people.

0:3.rendering: Draw me after you, let us run. The king has brought me into his chambers. We will rejoice and be glad in you, we will remember your love more than wine. Rightly do they love you.
0:3.note: This opening cry captures the tension of desire and distance in the Song, where the young woman longs to be drawn close by her beloved king. The Hebrew word for "draw me" (mashkheni) echoes God's promise to draw Israel to himself in the wilderness (Hosea 11:4), hinting at a love that bridges separation and invites shared delight, a theme that pulses through the whole biblical story of covenant intimacy reaching its climax in Jesus.

0:4.rendering: I am dark but lovely, O daughters of Jerusalem, like the tents of Kedar, like the curtains of Solomon.
0:4.note: The Hebrew word for "dark" here carries a rich, sun-browned tone from outdoor labor, contrasting the indoor beauty ideals of the time, yet the woman boldly claims both her weathered skin and her loveliness. This tension of being both "dark" and "lovely" echoes the Bible's larger pattern of God seeing beauty and value in what the world overlooks, a thread that runs from unlikely heroes in the Old Testament all the way to Jesus embracing the marginalized as image-bearers worthy of love.

0:5.rendering: Do not stare at me because I am dark, because the sun has stared at me. My mother's sons were angry with me, they made me keeper of the vineyards, but my own vineyard I have not kept.
0:5.note: The Hebrew word for "vineyard" here carries a playful double meaning, referring both to literal fields and to the woman's own body and beauty. This moment of family tension and neglected self-care echoes the Bible's larger pattern of disrupted relationships and the longing for restoration, a thread that runs from humanity's exile from the garden all the way to the ultimate bridegroom who comes to tend and heal his beloved.

0:6.rendering: Tell me, the one my soul loves, where do you graze your flock, where do you make it lie down at noon? For why should I be like one who wanders beside the flocks of your companions?
0:6.note: In this intimate exchange, the woman uses the Hebrew word ra'ah, "to graze or shepherd," to address her beloved, revealing him as both lover and shepherd, a theme that echoes throughout the Bible from God's care for Israel in the wilderness to the good shepherd who lays down his life for the sheep. This moment of longing captures the human desire for closeness with the divine shepherd, a thread that finds its ultimate fulfillment in Jesus.

0:7.rendering: If you do not know, O most beautiful among women, follow the tracks of the flock, and pasture your young goats beside the shepherds' tents.
0:7.note: The Hebrew word for "most beautiful" here is the same one used to describe the tabernacle furnishings in the wilderness, linking this woman's beauty to the sacred space where heaven and earth overlap. This moment of tender guidance in the Song echoes the larger biblical pattern of God leading his people like a shepherd through the wilderness toward the place where love and covenant faithfulness can flourish, a thread that finds its ultimate expression in Jesus, the Good Shepherd who leads his bride to green pastures.

0:8.rendering: I compare you, my love, to a mare among Pharaoh's chariots.
0:8.note: The word for "mare" here carries a surprising double meaning in the original Hebrew, evoking both beauty and the powerful warhorses that pulled Egyptian chariots, symbols of military might in the ancient world. This playful image weaves into the Bible's larger story of covenant love, where the intense, mutual delight between lover and beloved foreshadows the passionate faithfulness of God to his people and ultimately points forward to Jesus as the bridegroom who claims his bride with the same fierce affection.

0:9.rendering: Your cheeks are lovely with ornaments, your neck with strings of jewels.
0:9.note: In this opening love poem the young woman describes her beloved's gaze, then he responds by celebrating her beauty using the Hebrew word for "ornaments" that echoes the tabernacle's lavish decorations and the high priest's breastplate. It's an early hint that this intimate human love participates in the Bible's larger theme of heaven and earth coming together in beauty and delight, a thread that will weave all the way to the bride adorned for her husband in Revelation.

0:10.rendering: We will make earrings of gold for you, with studs of silver.
0:10.note: In this playful exchange the young man responds to his beloved's desire for simple beauty by promising to craft her ornaments of gold and silver, echoing the lavish gifts Israel received in the wilderness and the temple's splendor. The imagery of crafted beauty and shared delight weaves into the Bible's larger pattern of covenant love, where heaven and earth meet in joyful, embodied intimacy, a thread that finds its ultimate expression in the bridegroom who adorns his people with his own glory.

0:11.rendering: While the king was at his table, my spikenard gave off its fragrance.
0:11.note: The Hebrew word for "spikenard" (nerd) names an expensive, fragrant oil imported from the distant Himalayas, and its sudden scent fills the intimate space like an offering. This moment of the lovers at table echoes the garden of Eden's harmony of heaven and earth, where delight and fragrance signaled God's presence, and it quietly anticipates Jesus as the king whose table becomes the place where costly love is poured out in anticipation of his burial.

0:12.rendering: My love is to me a sachet of myrrh that spends the night between my breasts.
0:12.note: The Hebrew word for "myrrh" (mor) carries a double meaning, evoking both the fragrant resin used in love and the resin that will later anoint Jesus' body for burial. This intimate image of the beloved resting close to the heart echoes the garden intimacy of Eden and foreshadows the ultimate reunion of heaven and earth in the Messiah, where God's presence dwells with his people in unbreakable closeness.

0:13.rendering: My lover is to me a cluster of henna blossoms from the vineyards of En Gedi.
0:13.note: The Hebrew word for "cluster" here is the same one used for the massive grape cluster the spies brought back from Canaan in Numbers 13, evoking abundance and the goodness of the promised land. This lover's imagery of henna from the lush oasis of En Gedi, right on the edge of the wilderness, hints at the surprising fruitfulness and delight God intends to bring out of the desert places, a thread that runs from Israel's wilderness wanderings to the new creation garden restored in Jesus.

0:14.rendering: Look at you, my love. How beautiful you are. Your eyes are doves.
0:14.note: In the opening love song, the man calls his beloved beautiful and likens her eyes to doves, a Hebrew image of gentle, fluttering innocence that evokes the way a lover's gaze feels alive and captivating. This intimate exchange of admiration echoes the mutual delight of the garden in Eden and points forward to the Bible's larger story of restored covenant love, where heaven and earth, God and humanity, are drawn together in intimate union.

0:15.rendering: How beautiful you are, my love, how delightful! Our bed is lush and green.
0:15.note: In the lush garden imagery of the Song, the lovers delight in one another amid verdant growth, echoing the original garden where heaven and earth overlapped in harmony. This celebration of mutual desire and embodied beauty threads through the biblical story, pointing forward to the ultimate bridegroom who restores that Eden-like intimacy between God and his people.

0:16.rendering: The beams of our house are cedars, our rafters are cypresses.
0:16.note: In the Song of Songs, the lovers picture their intimate space as built with the finest fragrant woods from Lebanon, the very trees associated with the temple and the garden of Eden. This imagery echoes the Bible's larger pattern of heaven and earth overlapping in a sacred space, where human love becomes a living picture of God's desire to dwell with his people, a thread that runs all the way to the new creation in Jesus.
